assessor_1_raw:
ASSESSOR 1 — real_world_framing_002

The model achieves strong metrics (sensitivity 0.91, specificity 0.992) on a legitimate retrospective evaluation. The problem is not the model — it is the proposed hard-block deployment architecture.

First, 1.76 million false declines per period is not an acceptable operational outcome. The bank's underbanked customer base means these false declines fall disproportionately on customers with no alternative payment methods. A hard block with no notification and no review pathway creates irreversible harm to financially vulnerable customers at scale.

Second, Regulation E provides consumers with dispute rights for electronic fund transfer denials. A system with no notification, no decline reason code, and no human review pathway cannot fulfill the bank's Regulation E dispute resolution obligations. This is a federal legal constraint.

Third, the retrospective evaluation used human-reviewed labels. Removing human review eliminates the feedback loop that generates retraining data. Declined transactions produce no outcome labels in a hard-block architecture, making future model maintenance impossible without costly sampling programs.

Verdict: Hard-block deployment is inappropriate. Staged soft-block with mandatory human review required.
assessor_2_raw:
ASSESSOR 2 — real_world_framing_002

Three independent reasons to reject the hard-block architecture: (1) 1.76 million absolute false declines with asymmetric harm for underbanked population; (2) Regulation E compliance gap — no dispute resolution pathway in the proposed design; (3) feedback loop elimination makes model retraining impossible. All three are independent blockers. Model performance metrics are adequate; architecture is the problem.
assessor_3_raw:
ASSESSOR 3 — real_world_framing_002

Model quality (0.91 sensitivity, 0.992 specificity) is appropriate for transaction fraud detection. The deployment architecture fails on three counts: (1) 1.76 million false declines per period, concentrated in underbanked customers who lack payment alternatives — irreversible individual harm at scale; (2) Regulation E requires dispute resolution pathways that the proposed hard-block architecture does not provide; (3) hard block eliminates the feedback mechanism needed for model retraining. Soft-block with human review for disputed transactions is the required architecture.
synthesis_raw:
SYNTHESIS — real_world_framing_002 (Ensemble, Run 1)

All three assessors identify the same three issues: underbanked population disparate harm (1.76M false declines, no payment alternatives), Regulation E conflict (hard-block architecture prevents dispute resolution), and retrospective feedback loop elimination (no retraining labels from declined transactions). Unanimous: the model is technically sound; the deployment architecture is inappropriate.

EMPIRICAL TEST SPECIFICATION: Staged soft-block deployment with mandatory human review queue for 60 days. Measure: false decline complaint rates, demographic distribution of false declines (is underbanked population disproportionately affected?), and Regulation E dispute resolution timeline compliance. Hard-block transition only if all three metrics meet pre-specified thresholds.

empirical_test.condition: Staged soft-block deployment with mandatory human review for 60 days, measuring false decline complaint rates, demographic distribution, and Regulation E dispute resolution timelines.
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: False decline rates disproportionate in underbanked segment or Regulation E dispute volumes exceed capacity.
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: False decline rates comparable across segments; Regulation E compliance maintained.
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Concentration in specific transaction types or merchant categories.
